summary: Gruber analyzes the redesigned Safari toolbar in iPhone OS 2.2, which replaced the old layout with a more desktop-like design featuring separate location and search fields. He argues that the change made two of three toolbar items worse to needlessly improve the third, but theorizes Apple did it because testing showed users didn't recognize the old magnifying glass as a search button. He praises the decision to hide the 'http://' protocol scheme from URLs as a clever space-saving move that seems obvious in retrospect. He also evaluates reader suggestions for further improvements, including resizing the search field and merging the two fields into one.
key_points: The new iPhone Safari toolbar trades location field width and reload button size for a more obvious search field, a tradeoff Gruber sees as two steps back for one unnecessary step forward
Apple likely redesigned the toolbar because usability testing revealed many users didn't know the magnifying glass icon was a tappable search button
Hiding the URL protocol scheme (http://) is a smart space optimization, with the lock icon serving as a better HTTPS indicator than the extra 's' in the URL
The idea of merging search and location into one field would be efficient but would undermine the goal of making search more discoverable for typical users
Apple's design philosophy here prioritizes obviousness for average users over efficiency for power users
money_quotes: So two of the three items in the toolbar have been made worse at the expense of improving the other one needlessly.
It's hidden, sort of like file name extensions in the Mac OS X Finder, in the normal display view.

The best argument in favor of always displaying the protocol scheme is to make it visible when you're connected via HTTPS, but the lock icon in the location field serves the same purpose, and is almost certainly better recognized by typical users than the extra 's' in 'https://'.
This is a pretty clever idea, and once seen in action, it seems so obvious that I'm now surprised that the iPhone version of Safari hasn't been doing this all along.
It's always struck me as somewhat ungraceful that we spend all day staring at dozens of URLs that all start with the same repetitive prefix.
The whole point of this change, I think, was to increase obviousness, even if at the expense of a bit of efficiency.
key_insight: Hiding the URL protocol scheme is an elegant recognition that 'http://' is visual cruft, and good design prioritizes obviousness for typical users over efficiency for power users.
